items.title: Seven cases of hantavirus now identified on cruise ship stranded off west Africa
items.summary: Seven cases of the deadly hantavirus have now been identified in a suspected outbreak on a luxury cruise ship off West Africa. The outbreak has caused global concern, as the disease is considered rare among humans. What is happening with the ship, and what does hantavirus actually entail?

items.title: Does the Royal Commission into Antisemitism and Social Cohesion go far enough?
items.summary: The first two days of public hearings for the Royal Commission into Antisemitism and Social Cohesion has heard from 24 witnesses who are Jewish Australians with lived experience of antisemitism. While there is broad community support for a Royal Commission, some argue it should be expanded to include other minority perspectives including Muslim and First Nations Australians' experiences of racism.

Corporate headlines included a recall by A2 Milk in the United States, an ASIC investigation into Accent Group, and NAB’s weaker half-year profit driven by accounting changes despite solid lending growth and a steady dividend. items.title: INTERVIEW: North Aboriginal Justice Agency CEO accuses NT government of long term detention in watchhouses
items.summary: The Northern Territory justice system is under fresh scrutiny amid allegations people are being held in overcrowded cells for long periods of time without adequate healthcare in a Darwin correctional facility. A spokesperson for the Department of Corrections has said in a statement that the Darwin Transitional Custody Centre accommodates prisoners for a maximum of four weeks. They said prisoners undergo intake and medical screening to ensure they don't have any serious health concerns and are well suited to the environment. The spokesperson also said prisoners do have access to phones and can contact family. But this week, the North Australia Aboriginal Justice Agency (NAAJA) accused the government of failing to fully address the issue, saying parts of the Darwin City Watchhouse had been renamed and transferred to the control of the corrections department – while conditions continue to resemble a watchhouse. NAAJA CEO Ben Grimes is being interviewed here by Tee Mitchell.

items.title: ‘Songs in Sign Language’ reimagines Disney musical moments in ASL
items.summary: Disney Plus has released Disney Animation's Songs in Sign Language, which reimagines three animated musical sequences from recent feature films. In this episode of SBS On the Money, Morningstar’s Lochlan Halloway unpacks the day’s market action, as ANZ posts a higher profit and signals confidence Australia will avoid recession. Meanwhile, Coles flags further price pressures across supermarket shelves, and Cotality’s Tim Lawless warns Australia’s housing market may be entering a downturn as interest rates bite and growth slows.

The effect of rising oil prices is being reflected in company reports with Woolworths Group downgrading its full-year profit outlook, despite reporting a lift in third quarter food sales. For more, Stephanie Youssef spoke with TMS Private Wealth Portfolio Manager Ben Clark.

items.title: The first report into an antisemitism Royal Commission has been published. What does it say?
items.summary: The Royal Commission on Antisemitism and Social Cohesion has tabled its interim report, just two months after the commission was launched. Lead commissioner Virginia Bell says the commission has not identified any security issues requiring urgent or immediate action. But the report makes fourteen initial recommendations, including the implementation of nationally consistent gun laws.

Despite claims of military success, divisions are growing in Congress over the cost, strategy and long-term consequences of the war.

The report from the John Curtin Research Centre argues that AI’s impact on Australian workers won’t be decided by technology, but by political choices.

items.title: Inflation surges as rate bets rise and markets continue to slide
items.summary: A sharp lift in inflation and a seventh straight fall on the ASX set the tone for this episode of the SBS On the Money podcast, unpacking how global conflict is feeding into Australia’s economy. Official data shows annual inflation expanding to 4.6 per cent in March, driven by a surge in fuel and electricity costs. Harry Murphy Cruise from Oxford Economics breaks down what this means for interest rates and how much further prices could rise, while Damien Boey from Wilson Asset Management explains the market reaction, sector moves and what investors are watching next as US tech earnings come into focus.

items.title: Penny Wong warns IS-linked families face 'full force of the law' if they return to Australia
items.summary: The foreign minister reiterated that her government will not assist their return as she prepares to tour Asia to shore up energy supplies.

items.title: Gaza just held its first vote in 20 years. What did it reveal?
items.summary: Gaza's first election in two decades saw low turnout in Deir al-Balah, as Fatah-backed candidates secured most seats.

items.title: 40 years on from the Chernobyl disaster, leaders draw parallels with then and now
items.summary: Ukraine's President Volodymyr Zelenskyy has met with his Moldovan counterpart in Kyiv, as the world marks the 40th anniversary of the Chernobyl nuclear disaster- and draws stark contrasts with the current state of play in Europe.

items.summary: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

items.title: Sabastian Sawe makes history by breaking two-hour marathon barrier in London
items.summary: Kenya's Sabastian Sawe has become the first person to shatter the fabled two-hour barrier in the marathon with a breathtaking win in London.

items.title: INTERVIEW: Professor Margie Danchin on falling vaccination rates
items.summary: A paediatrician says misinformation about childhood vaccines coming out of the United States has led to a huge spike in vaccine hesitancy in Australia in the past year. Professor Margie Danchin from the Murdoch Children's Research Institute says the latest figures show Australia has the lowest vaccine coverage for children under 5 in more than a decade, with the rates falling each year since 2020. She spoke with Greg Dyett.

items.title: Thirty years after Port Arthur, gun numbers in Australia are higher than ever
items.summary: Thirty years after the Port Arthur massacre, rising gun numbers are testing Australia's gun laws and public safety approach.

items.title: 'Serious consequences': Universities given fresh edict on Iran, Russia and North Korea
items.summary: One university said its warning to staff came after it had received "updated advice" from the federal government.

items.title: Sandra will never own a home. Meet the boomers struggling in the housing crisis
items.summary: While baby boomers still dominate property ownership in Australia, the rate has gone down. Some have been forced to find alternatives.

items.title: Palestinian elections give some in Gaza first chance to vote in two decades
items.summary: Palestinians voted in local elections, the first in two decades in Gaza and the first in the occupied West Bank since the start of the Israel-Hamas war.

items.title: Hanson says she is 'not against migration at all' as Taylor warns of threats from 'bad countries'
items.summary: Pauline Hanson has addressed crowds at an anti-immigration rally in Canberra, just hours after Opposition leader Angus Taylor doubled down on his freshly-unveiled immigration policy.

items.title: Chernobyl, 40 years on: Survivors, silence and an exclusion zone
items.summary: A rally in Sydney will mark four decades since the Chernobyl nuclear accident, amid warnings of ongoing nuclear risks in Ukraine.

items.title: 'Talking about nothing': Peace talks in limbo as Trump cancels envoys' Pakistan trip
items.summary: Trump said he called off the planned visit by his envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner because the talks in Islamabad involved too much travel and expense.

items.title: Are migrant communities and non-citizen taxpayers being forgotten in cost of living conversations?
items.summary: The war in the Middle East has impacted the cost of living in Australia, affecting communities across the country. Representatives for migrant and refugee communities are concerned needs are not adequately being addressed however, as economic and mental health impacts combine.

items.title: 'Carrying things that aren't mine': Anxiety in the age of constant crisis
items.summary: Constant exposure to global crises is fuelling anxiety and reshaping how people stay informed and when they step back.

items.title: Sergio suspected his wife had been unfaithful. So he turned to 'love detectives'
items.summary: In Peru, divorce is on the rise and infidelity can impact the division of assets. Private detectives like Liz play an important role.

items.title: Ultra-processed foods damage your focus and may increase dementia risk, study finds
items.summary: Even small increases in ultra-processed foods can lead to poorer brain function, regardless of diet quality, a new study has found.

items.title: Indigenous leaders, politicians and military chiefs condemn Anzac Day booing
items.summary: Booing disrupted Welcome to Country addresses at Anzac Day dawn services in Melbourne, Perth and Sydney where a man was arrested.

items.title: Meet the military veteran creating comfort and connection through chai
items.summary: A veteran brews more than chai. He's stirring connection, culture and community one sip at a time.

items.title: Fighting for a country that denied their rights: Remembering the service of Australia's Indigenous veterans
items.summary: [[WARNING: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people should exercise caution when listening to this audio as it contains names and images of deceased persons, which may cause distress to members of these communities.]] Since the First World War, Indigenous men and women have served in Australia's defence forces, despite the government's refusal to recognise their rights. NITV Radio's Kerri-Lee Barry has spoken to Michael Bell about members of her own family who served in Australia's defence forces.

items.title: ‘Tea with heart’ – meet the military veteran giving back to others who serve
items.summary: A chain of specialty tea bars founded by a military veteran is expanding rapidly. Ambi's Chai serves tea blended with exotic spices - and its founder is proud his cafes are bringing communities closer.

items.title: Fuel prices under threat, jobs transforming — and a new tax break for Australians
items.summary: The key money stories this week, broken down simply and what they mean for you.

items.title: Amid war crimes investigations, some veterans are grappling with the Anzac legend
items.summary: As Anzac commemorations are held across the country, many are reflecting on the legacy of service in the wake of alleged war crimes.

items.title: Government says 'no change' in position amid growing calls for 25 per cent gas export tax
items.summary: Independent senator Allegra Spender said a 25 per cent tax on gas exports would help fix issues in the sector.

items.title: The women facing 'impossible choices' as a crisis grips Australia
items.summary: Cost of living pressures are thought to be exacerbating instances of domestic and family violence.

items.title: Markets slip as tech earnings loom and trust in finfluencers questioned
items.summary: The ASX200 edged lower by 0.1 per cent, capping a weekly fall of 1.8 per cent, as global markets pulled back from record highs and investors turned their attention to upcoming tech earnings, with Tony Sycamore from IG Markets unpacking what’s ahead. Meanwhile, new data from ASIC reveals a growing number of young Australians are turning to AI platforms, social media and so-called finfluencers for financial information, raising concerns about credibility and oversight. ASIC Commissioner Alan Kirkland explains the regulator’s response.

items.title: A Trump envoy said Italy should replace Iran at the World Cup. Then things kicked off
items.summary: Iran and Italy have reacted strongly to suggestions that Italy could be substituted for Iran at the World Cup.

items.summary: The Australian Federal Police is aware of the campaign that has been promoted by the Iranian embassy in Canberra.

items.title: For Gen Z, dumb phones and CDs are the winners as they return to analog
items.summary: Analog devices are making a comeback with the most tech-savvy, plugged in generation of all time - Gen Z. Why are Gen Z using tech from a time they don't remember?

items.title: INTERVIEW: Will things ever return to 'normal' in the Strait of Hormuz?
items.summary: Shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz remains at a virtual standstill with President Trump ordering US forces to 'shoot and kill' Iranian vessels laying mines in the Strait. The US military says it seized another tanker associated with smuggling Iranian oil in the Indian Ocean. The U.S. and Iran had declared the Strait open on Friday April 17 but Iran reimposed control on Saturday. Tehran said it was responding to a continued U.S. blockade of Iranian ports, calling it a violation of their ceasefire. So will we ever see a return to normal shipping and trade, once the strait reopens? In this episode of Weekend One on One, we hear from Alexis Ellender, senior lead analyst at maritime data firm Kpler

items.title: UK to ban everyone born after 2008 from buying cigarettes
items.summary: The British health secretary says the move will create a "smoke-free" generation.

items.title: Israel-Lebanon confirm three-week ceasefire extension after White House negotiations
items.summary: Thursday marked the most significant test of the ceasefire yet, after Israeli strikes killed a Lebanese journalist.

items.title: Australia's Twelve Apostles draw millions. Now, their mysteries have been solved
items.summary: The enigma of Australia's iconic limestone stacks has been uncovered.

items.title: Employers seeking more workers with AI skills, ASX pulls further away from record
items.summary: The ASX slipped 0.6 per cent amid rising geopolitical tensions in the Gulf, with losses led by materials and the big banks, while energy stocks climbed on a surge in oil prices. Against that backdrop, a sharp shift in the jobs market is emerging, with SEEK reporting a 75 per cent rise in advertised roles mentioning AI skills.

items.title: Advocates cheer decision to make showers free for home-based aged care
items.summary: The lion's share of attention this week has been on the National Disability Insurance Scheme, with the federal government announcing cuts that it's estimated will see tens of thousands removed from the scheme. But there have also been developments in aged care too- the federal government has now reversed its policy on the funding for showering after a mass of complaints from residents, families, and advocates.

items.title: TACO Trump? An idiot’s guide to Strait talking & Marcos Jnr gym junkie
items.summary: Confusion deepens on the Strait of Hormuz as President Trump extends the ceasefire with Iran. Another truce hangs in the balance with Israel and Hezbollah trading accusations - while thousands of Israeli troops draw a line in southern Lebanon. Plus, Japan loosens its long-standing arms export restrictions and world leaders flex their fitness credentials.

items.title: Who holds the power? The key question behind Trump's latest Iran demand
items.summary: The US president wants a "unified" response — but some argue there could be "division" among Iranian officials on a potential peace deal.

items.title: Iran seizes ships in Strait of Hormuz in escalation of naval blockade
items.summary: Iran has seized two ships crossing the Strait of Hormuz. It says it won't reopen the crucial waterway while a US blockade of its vessels and ports remains in place.

items.title: Who gets a public holiday for Anzac Day across Australia?
items.summary: Anzac Day falls on a Saturday this year, with new rule changes coming into effect for the first time.

items.title: INTERVIEW: Why have the ceasefire talks stalled?
items.summary: Ceasefire talks between the US and Iran seem to be on hold. Pakistan had planned to host a second round of talks, but the White House suspended Vice President JD Vance’s planned trip to Islamabad - as Iran rebuffed efforts to restart negotiations. Pakistani officials say authorities will keep security arrangements in place in Islamabad in case US and Iranian delegations ultimately arrive. Speaking to the Associated Press, Qamar Cheema, the Executive Director of the Sanober Institute in Islamabad, says talks between the U.S. and Iran have stalled due to a lack of clarity and a complex power struggle within Tehran

items.title: Iran seizes two ships crossing Hormuz, says it won't reopen strait while US blockade remains
items.summary: Iran's Revolutionary Guards said their naval forces had seized two container ships trying to cross the strategic strait.

items.title: 'Can no longer be stretched': The 'defining shift' that has renters at their limits
items.summary: Sky-high rents persist, but price growth is slowing and diverging across Australia's capitals, according to a report.

items.title: ‘That future was gone’: The pain of losing your life partner young
items.summary: The death of a partner can feel debilitating at any age, but in your 20s and 30s, it brings unique challenges.

items.title: Online watchdog puts video game giants on notice as child grooming concerns rise
items.summary: The news comes after Roblox recently introduced new account types for underage users as pressure from global regulators mounted.

items.title: Iranian gunboat fires at container ship off Oman, ship off Iran also targeted
items.summary: The vessel had been sailing under a Liberian flag and "had been informed it had permission to transit the Strait of Hormuz."

items.title: Super funds recover March fall as middle east war impacts company earnings
items.summary: The Australian sharemarket slipped 1.1 per cent, yet remains within reach of a record high, continuing its recovery from March’s downturn as super funds rebound and long-term gains hold steady. In this episode of SBS On the Money, Ricardo Gonçalves unpacks the day’s biggest moves, including Cochlear’s dramatic 40 per cent fall to a 10-year low after a profit warning tied to weak US consumer confidence and global uncertainty. Portfolio manager James Gerrish from Market Partners joins the conversation to discuss whether other companies will also be hit by the war in the Middle-East.

items.title: Woolworths executive says 'prices dropped' rules changed as inflation grew
items.summary: The ACCC claims the discounted prices were often the same or higher than the original shelf prices.

items.title: With a degenerative disability, Jane is unsure of her future. Now she's fearful of NDIS cuts
items.summary: Disability advocates have flagged concerns that an overhaul of the NDIS scheme could disproportionately impact parts of the community.

items.title: 'We're the mugs here': Is Japan getting Australian gas for free?
items.summary: Discussions on Australia's gas and oil resources are heating up as a 25 per cent gas export tax gains traction.

items.title: SBS World News talks to Immigration Minister Tony Burke
items.summary: SBS News political reporter Anna Henderson speaks to Immigration Minister Tony Burke about visas, the War in the Middle East, and IS brides. Mr Burke says federal Opposition Leader Angus Taylor is trying to turn Australians against one another with his policies.

items.title: The 'horrific' truth about the romance scammers targeting vulnerable Australians
items.summary: Behind the everyday scams targeting Australians, there are scores of vulnerable victims trafficked into the job.

items.title: The US-Iran ceasefire has been extended. But were the warring states ever in agreement?
items.summary: As a two-week ceasefire appears to be extended, experts ask whether there was any agreement in the first place.

items.title: Pressure builds to lift Australia's 'tiny' gas tax
items.summary: As calls mount for a 25 per cent tax on gas exports, a leading economist has trashed the existing measures at a Senate inquiry.

items.title: Markets pause, as Apple turns the page on Tim Cook's era
items.summary: The ASX 200 edged lower as investors held back ahead of a key Middle East ceasefire deadline, with Michael Wayne from Medallion Financial Group unpacking the day’s market moves. At the same time, Apple enters a new chapter as Tim Cook steps down after 15 years, having overseen explosive growth and the launch of products like the Apple Watch and AirPods. Stephanie Youssef speaks with Wedbush Securities’ Dan Ives about Cook’s legacy, the challenges facing incoming CEO John Ternus, and why artificial intelligence could define Apple’s next phase.

items.title: SBS World News talks to Assistant Minister for Citizenship Julian Hill
items.summary: SBS federal political reporters Anna Henderson, Rayane Tamer, and Naveen Razik have an in-depth conversation with Assistant Minister for Citizenship, Customs, and Multicultural Affairs, Julian Hill, about visa categories, and the task the government faces in dealing with humanitarian visas and family reunion.

items.title: A $71bn rebuild and 77-year setback: A 'catastrophic' crisis in the Middle East
items.summary: The cost of rebuilding Gaza and the "catastrophic" human toll of war have been laid bare in a new report.

items.summary: A daily five minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

items.title: NASA rolls out core stage for the next mission to the moon: Artemis III
items.summary: It's been a week since the crew of Artemis II (2) dropped back to earth after a record-breaking mission, but concrete steps are already being taken to achieve the next phase of NASA's lunar goals. The Michoud Assembly Facility in New Orleans has rolled out the largest section of NASA's Space Launch System rocket, which will launch a second crewed mission known as Artemis III (3).

items.title: Already under pressure, Australia’s food system could now be in big trouble
items.summary: Australia should be a rich global food bowl, but the future is looking more uncertain — for a range of reasons.

items.title: Inside the 'military operation' of one of Australia's rarest parenting experiences
items.summary: Parents of triplets face heavy financial and emotional pressures. Are Australia's support systems built for families like theirs?

items.title: Tsunami warning issued after powerful earthquake strikes off northern Japan coast
items.summary: Buildings as far away as Tokyo shook during the tremor, and authorities warned further quakes could strike in coming days.

items.title: $2 petrol prices likely here to stay, as government looks to new instant tax deduction
items.summary: Energy stocks lag, as oil prices remained volatile around supply concerns in the Strait of Hormuz, even as petrol prices eased, though still well above last year’s levels. Market analyst Evan Lucas unpacks what it all means for households and investors, as attention also turns to the federal government’s new $1,000 instant tax deduction set to benefit more than six million workers. Mark Chapman from H&R Block explains who gains from that, how it works, and when the savings will flow through.

items.title: Free RSV vaccinations for older Australians
items.summary: The Federal Government has announced free R-S-V vaccinations for older Australians, in a push to better protect those most at risk from the potentially deadly respiratory virus. The $445 million dollar investment means Australians aged 75 and over - and Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people aged 60 and over - will be able to access the immunisation for free from mid May, saving them as much as $300 dollars per vaccination.

items.title: Pope Leo is celebrated as he visits Angola
items.summary: Pope Leo’s Africa tour is drawing large turnouts and warm welcomes. In Angola, the mood is celebratory, while in Washington D-C, not so much, as Pope Leo addresses the US President's criticism of him.

items.summary: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

items.title: Cash was meant to be dying. But new RBA data tells a different story
items.summary: Cash use ticked up for the first time since 2007 — and one in three Australians say they'd struggle without it, according to a survey.

items.title: 'We haven't learned anything': A looming 'crunch' for Australia's food system
items.summary: Australia's approach to fertiliser import disruptions means doubling down on a fragile, fracturing system, some experts say.

items.title: Over 60 per cent of popular Cheaper Home Batteries Program installations 'substandard'
items.summary: A new regulatory report says the installation problems it found risk not only the credibility of the scheme, but also public safety.

items.title: 'One thing after another': The almost $20,000 penalty for being single in Australia
items.summary: Being single comes at a "premium" in Australia, leaving those not in a relationship thousands of dollars worse off.

items.title: Australians cut back on stockpiling petrol, reduce fuel spend as households adjust
items.summary: New analysis from a major bank shows Australians have cut their spending on fuel and are going back to filling up when they need it, rather than panic buying.

items.title: 'Damn it, let's go after him': What the Democrats need to topple Donald Trump
items.summary: The midterm elections are on the horizon, and the contest to succeed Donald Trump is quietly underway.

items.title: Twenty protesters arrested for violating Queensland's ban on pro-Palestinian phrases
items.summary: Those who recite or display "from the river to the sea" or "globalise the intifada" in Queensland can face up to two years' jail.

items.title: While the world's eyes were on the moon, Australia quietly axed a major space deal
items.summary: The end of a significant membership has some wondering whether Australia's days as an astronomy powerhouse are numbered.

items.title: 'It impacted the relationship with my culture': When culture, cuisine and a chronic illness combine
items.summary: Coeliac disease affects one in seventy Australians, according to the Wesley Research Institute. But when food is integral to cultural identity, the impacts of this autoimmune disease — especially on multicultural and diasporic communities in Australia — can extend beyond the physical, affecting mental health. Jude Soussan is a young person of Lebanese heritage in Perth, Western Australia, and has used her diagnosis to help educate others about what it means to live with coeliac disease in the diaspora, using creativity. She hopes that, with time, communities can better understand how to accommodate chronic illness.

items.title: 'Borrowing from the past': This old way of living is making a comeback in Australia
items.summary: My grandparents' home is my home too, and that's becoming more common in Australia.
